title: The Yeast Guide
subtitle: Fermentation, strains, and the invisible winemaker

overview.title: The Invisible Winemaker
overview.description: Every wine is made twice: once by the grape and once by yeast. These single-celled fungi transform sugar into alcohol, but they do far more than that. Yeast produces hundreds of flavor compounds, esters, aldehydes, and organic acids that shape a wine's character as profoundly as terroir or winemaking decisions. Understanding yeast is understanding fermentation itself.

role_in_winemaking.primary_function: Convert grape sugars (glucose and fructose) into ethanol and carbon dioxide through anaerobic respiration
secondary_functions: Produce flavor compounds (esters, higher alcohols, aldehydes)
Contribute to mouthfeel through mannoproteins and polysaccharides
Influence color stability in red wines
Produce glycerol (adds body and sweetness perception)
Release thiols from bound precursors (tropical aromas)
Metabolize organic acids (some strains reduce malic acid)
role_in_winemaking.the_equation: C6H12O6 → 2 C2H5OH + 2 CO2 (Glucose → Ethanol + Carbon Dioxide)
role_in_winemaking.yield: Approximately 0.51g ethanol and 0.49g CO2 per gram of sugar (theoretical maximum)

the_biochemistry.glycolysis: Glucose is broken down through 10 enzymatic steps to pyruvate, generating 2 ATP per glucose molecule
the_biochemistry.alcoholic_fermentation: Pyruvate is decarboxylated to acetaldehyde by pyruvate decarboxylase, then reduced to ethanol by alcohol dehydrogenase, regenerating NAD+
the_biochemistry.crabtree_effect: Even in the presence of oxygen, S. cerevisiae preferentially ferments rather than respires when glucose exceeds 0.1%. This is why wine ferments rather than 'spoiling' aerobically.
the_biochemistry.heat_generation: Fermentation releases approximately 23.5 kcal per mole of glucose. A 1000L tank can rise 1.3°C per 1° Brix drop without cooling.
the_biochemistry.efficiency: In practice, yields are 0.45-0.48g ethanol per gram sugar due to biomass production and side reactions

wild_vs_cultured_debate.title: The Great Debate
wild_vs_cultured_debate.description: No topic divides winemakers more than yeast selection. The choice between wild (indigenous, native, spontaneous) fermentation and cultured (commercial, inoculated) yeast reflects fundamental philosophies about winemaking.

wild_advocates.argument: Wild yeasts express terroir. Each vineyard has a unique microbiome that contributes to site-specific character.
wild_advocates.proponents: Natural wine movement, many Burgundy producers, traditional estates in Loire, some biodynamic producers
notable_practitioners: Domaine de la Romanée-Conti (Burgundy) - exclusively indigenous fermentation
Nicolas Joly at Coulée de Serrant (Loire) - wild fermentation pioneer
Gravner (Friuli) - amphora fermentation with indigenous yeasts

cultured_advocates.argument: Commercial yeasts provide consistency, reliability, and specific flavor profiles. Wild fermentation is gambling.
cultured_advocates.proponents: Most large-scale producers, New World wineries, anyone prioritizing predictability
notable_practitioners: Most Champagne houses - require consistent secondary fermentation
Large California producers - consistency at scale
Australian volume producers - reliability in warm climates

the_third_way.description: Many premium producers use hybrid approaches, capturing benefits of both philosophies
approaches: Indigenous start, commercial finish: Let wild yeasts establish complexity, inoculate at 5-8% alcohol to ensure completion
Lot separation: Indigenous for best parcels, commercial for others, blend for complexity
Pied de cuve: Build indigenous starter before harvest for more reliable wild fermentation
Non-Saccharomyces inoculation: Use commercial non-Sacch for early phase, then S. cerevisiae
notable_practitioners: Ridge Vineyards (California) - documented native yeast populations since 1970s
Henschke (Eden Valley) - pied de cuve tradition
Many Burgundy négociants - blend approaches by cuvée

the_science.vineyard_vs_winery: Research shows most fermenting yeasts come from winery equipment, not vineyards. The 'winery microbiome' may matter more than the vineyard microbiome.
the_science.studies: Goddard et al. (2010) found S. cerevisiae rarely present on healthy grapes; most comes from winery-resident populations
the_science.implications: Indigenous fermentation may express 'winery terroir' rather than vineyard terroir

wild_vs_cultured_debate.reality: Many winemakers use hybrid approaches: indigenous fermentation to start, cultured yeast to finish; or different yeasts for different lots.

winery_microbiome.title: The Winery Microbiome
winery_microbiome.description: Every winery develops its own resident yeast population over years of operation. These winery-adapted strains colonize equipment, walls, and floors.
winery_microbiome.formation: Takes 3-5 years for a new winery to develop a stable, dominant S. cerevisiae population
characteristics: Winery strains are pre-adapted to wine conditions (high sugar, low pH, alcohol)
Old wineries may have unique strains found nowhere else
Strains persist in cracks in concrete, wooden equipment, drains
Summer cleaning can disrupt populations; some producers avoid aggressive sanitization
famous_examples: Château Musar (Lebanon) - claims indigenous yeasts surviving since 1930s
Domaine de la Romanée-Conti - documented unique strains in their cellars
Penfolds (Australia) - resident strains in historic cellars

history.title: A History of Fermentation
history.description: Humans have made wine for 8,000 years without knowing yeast existed. The discovery that fermentation was caused by living organisms, not spontaneous chemistry, revolutionized winemaking.

timeline.event: Earliest evidence of winemaking in Georgia (Caucasus). Qvevri fermentation relies on wild yeasts.
timeline.significance: Humanity makes wine for millennia without understanding fermentation

timeline.event: Egyptian hieroglyphics depict winemaking. Wine production spreads through Mediterranean.
timeline.significance: Fermentation technology spreads without scientific understanding

timeline.event: Antonie van Leeuwenhoek observes yeast cells under microscope, calls them 'globules.'
timeline.significance: First observation of yeast, but their role in fermentation unknown

timeline.event: Antoine Lavoisier quantifies fermentation, showing sugar converts to alcohol and CO2 in fixed proportions.
timeline.significance: First chemical understanding of fermentation, though mechanism unknown

timeline.event: Charles Cagniard de la Tour, Theodor Schwann, and Friedrich Kützing independently conclude that yeast are living organisms responsible for fermentation.
timeline.significance: The biological theory of fermentation begins to replace spontaneous generation

timeline.event: Louis Pasteur proves fermentation is caused by living yeast cells, not chemical reaction. Publishes 'Mémoire sur la fermentation alcoolique.'
timeline.significance: The birth of modern fermentation science. Pasteur calls fermentation 'life without air'

timeline.event: Emil Christian Hansen at Carlsberg brewery isolates pure yeast strains for consistent brewing.
timeline.significance: The beginning of strain selection and inoculation

timeline.event: Hermann Müller-Thurgau at Geisenheim Research Institute begins systematic study of wine yeasts in Germany.
timeline.significance: European wine science institutionalizes yeast research

timeline.event: Eduard Buchner demonstrates cell-free fermentation using yeast extract, proving enzymes (not living cells) drive the chemistry. Wins 1907 Nobel Prize.
timeline.significance: Birth of biochemistry. Enzymes like zymase identified as the active agents

timeline.event: UC Davis (then University Farm) established. Begins California wine research program.
timeline.significance: Foundation of American wine science

timeline.event: Embden-Meyerhof-Parnas pathway (glycolysis) fully elucidated.
timeline.significance: Complete biochemical understanding of fermentation achieved

timeline.event: Maynard Amerine joins UC Davis, begins systematic study of wine yeasts and fermentation.
timeline.significance: Amerine becomes 'father of modern American wine science'

timeline.event: UC Davis develops freeze-dried yeast cultures for distribution.
timeline.significance: First practical method for transporting viable yeast

timeline.event: UC Davis releases Montrachet strain (UCD 522), its first commercial wine yeast.
timeline.significance: American wine industry standardizes on cultured yeasts

timeline.event: Lallemand develops commercial Active Dry Yeast (ADY) production for wine.
timeline.significance: Yeast becomes shelf-stable, affordable, globally distributable

timeline.event: Killer yeast phenomenon characterized. Competition between strains understood.
timeline.significance: Explains why some indigenous fermentations fail; leads to killer-positive commercial strains

timeline.event: S. cerevisiae genome fully sequenced (first eukaryote).
timeline.significance: Enables genetic understanding of fermentation traits

timeline.event: Natural wine movement emerges in France, embracing wild fermentation.
timeline.significance: Counter-movement rejects cultured yeast dominance

timeline.event: First commercial non-Saccharomyces products launched (Torulaspora delbrueckii).
timeline.significance: Non-Saccharomyces yeasts move from 'spoilage' to 'tool'

timeline.event: ML01, first GMO wine yeast, approved in USA and Canada. Performs MLF without bacteria.
timeline.significance: GMO enters wine, though adoption remains minimal due to consumer resistance

timeline.event: Rapid expansion of non-Saccharomyces products. Sequential inoculation becomes mainstream for aromatic whites.
timeline.significance: The third way: controlled use of 'wild' species

timeline.event: Climate change drives research into low-alcohol yeasts and acid-producing strains.
timeline.significance: Yeast selection becomes climate adaptation strategy

pasteur.name: Louis Pasteur
pasteur.contribution: Proved that fermentation is a biological process performed by living yeast cells, not a spontaneous chemical reaction
key_experiments: Demonstrated that boiled (sterile) grape juice does not ferment until yeast is introduced
Showed that different microorganisms cause different types of fermentation
Identified oxygen as detrimental to alcoholic fermentation ('Pasteur effect')
Used swan-neck flasks to disprove spontaneous generation
Identified bacteria responsible for wine spoilage (acetic acid bacteria, lactic acid bacteria)
pasteur.studies_on_wine: Études sur le Vin (1866) - comprehensive treatise on wine diseases and their microbial causes
pasteur.famous_quote: In the fields of observation, chance favors only the prepared mind.
pasteur.legacy: Founded microbiology, enabled controlled fermentation, made modern winemaking possible
pasteur.pasteurization: Developed heat treatment (pasteurization) to kill spoilage organisms in wine, though rarely used in fine wine today

other_pioneers.name: Emil Christian Hansen
other_pioneers.contribution: Developed pure culture technique for isolating single yeast strains. First to produce beer from a single-cell isolate (1883).
other_pioneers.legacy: Father of industrial yeast selection. Carlsberg strain naming convention still used.

other_pioneers.name: Eduard Buchner
other_pioneers.contribution: Demonstrated cell-free fermentation using ground yeast extract (zymase). Proved enzymes, not living cells, perform fermentation chemistry.
other_pioneers.legacy: Nobel Prize in Chemistry 1907. Founded biochemistry as a discipline.

other_pioneers.name: Hermann Müller-Thurgau
other_pioneers.contribution: Systematic study of wine yeasts. Developed grape crossing techniques. Bred Müller-Thurgau grape variety.
other_pioneers.legacy: Established German wine science tradition. Geisenheim remains leading yeast research center.

other_pioneers.name: Maynard Amerine
other_pioneers.contribution: Established scientific winemaking curriculum. Developed climate classification system. Systematically studied yeast strains for California conditions.
other_pioneers.legacy: 'Father of American wine science.' Trained generation of California winemakers. Authored definitive textbooks.

other_pioneers.name: Ralph Kunkee
other_pioneers.contribution: Pioneered malolactic fermentation research. First to isolate and characterize ML bacteria for commercial use.
other_pioneers.legacy: Made MLF a controlled process rather than chance occurrence.

species.title: Yeast Species
species.description: Wine fermentation involves multiple yeast species. Saccharomyces cerevisiae dominates, but non-Saccharomyces yeasts play crucial roles in wild fermentations and are increasingly used intentionally.

succession.title: Yeast Succession in Wild Fermentation
succession.description: In spontaneous fermentation, different species dominate at different stages. This succession creates complexity but also risk.

stages.notes: Apiculate yeasts dominate. High populations on grape skins. Produce esters but also volatile acidity.
Transition phase. Non-Saccharomyces begin dying off. S. cerevisiae populations building.
S. cerevisiae takes over. More alcohol-tolerant. Outcompetes other species.
Only Saccharomyces survives. Completes fermentation to dryness.

succession.timing: The entire succession typically occurs over 7-21 days depending on temperature and conditions.

killer_factor.title: Killer Yeasts
killer_factor.description: Some yeast strains produce toxins that kill other yeasts. This 'killer factor' affects both wild and inoculated fermentations.
killer_factor.mechanism: Killer yeasts secrete protein toxins (K1, K2, K28 types) that disrupt cell walls of sensitive strains.

categories.killer: Produces toxin and is immune to it. K+ strains dominate mixed fermentations.
categories.sensitive: Killed by toxin. May die even if inoculated at high populations.
categories.neutral: Neither produces toxin nor is affected by it.

implications: Indigenous fermentations often have killer-positive wild strains that eliminate inoculated yeast
Commercial killer-positive strains (EC-1118, K1-V1116) resist wild yeast competition
Stuck fermentations sometimes caused by killer yeast eliminating the primary strain
Restart yeasts should be killer-positive to overcome resident populations

items.name: Saccharomyces cerevisiae
items.description: The workhorse of fermentation. Highly alcohol-tolerant, vigorous, reliable. All commercial wine yeast strains are Saccharomyces cerevisiae, selected for specific traits.
characteristics: High alcohol tolerance (14-18%+ depending on strain)
items.origin: Ubiquitous in nature, found on grape skins, in wineries, on equipment
items.flavor_contribution: Strain-dependent: from neutral to highly aromatic

items.name: Saccharomyces bayanus
items.description: Closely related to S. cerevisiae, now often classified as a subspecies. Exceptional stress tolerance makes it ideal for difficult fermentations and secondary fermentation in sparkling wine.
characteristics: Often used to restart stuck fermentations
items.origin: Isolated from Champagne and other sparkling wine regions

items.name: Brettanomyces bruxellensis
items.description: The most controversial yeast in wine. Produces volatile phenols (4-ethylphenol, 4-ethylguaiacol) that create 'barnyard,' 'band-aid,' and 'horse stable' aromas. Considered a fault by most, a terroir expression by some.
characteristics: Can continue activity at very low sugar levels
Thrives in barrels, difficult to eradicate
items.origin: Winery-resident, difficult to eliminate once established

controversy.fault_view: Masks varietal character, indicates poor hygiene
controversy.character_view: Low levels add complexity, express winery terroir
controversy.regional_tolerance: More accepted in Rhône, some traditional Bordeaux; rejected in most New World

items.name: Torulaspora delbrueckii
items.description: A 'friendly' non-Saccharomyces increasingly used commercially. Produces glycerol and polysaccharides that enhance mouthfeel, while generating interesting ester profiles. Often used in co-fermentation with S. cerevisiae.
items.pairing: Co-inoculated with S. cerevisiae, which takes over as alcohol rises

items.name: Metschnikowia pulcherrima
items.description: Naturally present early in wild fermentations. Releases thiols (tropical aromas) from grape precursors more effectively than many S. cerevisiae strains. Increasingly used for Sauvignon Blanc.
characteristics: Needs S. cerevisiae to complete fermentation
items.commercial_use: Flavia (Chr. Hansen), used for Sauvignon Blanc

items.name: Lachancea thermotolerans
items.description: Produces lactic acid during fermentation, naturally acidifying wine without malolactic fermentation. Increasingly valuable for warm-climate wines that lack natural acidity.
items.climate_change_relevance: Helps address declining acidity in warming regions

items.name: Pichia kluyveri
items.description: Present early in wild fermentations. Produces high levels of esters, particularly fruity and floral compounds. Used to enhance aromatic complexity.
characteristics: Must be followed by S. cerevisiae

items.name: Schizosaccharomyces pombe
items.description: Unlike most yeasts (budding), S. pombe divides by fission. Its unique metabolism converts malic acid to ethanol, naturally deacidifying wine. Used in warm regions or high-acid vintages.
items.flavor_contribution: Neutral, mainly used for acid reduction

items.name: Hanseniaspora uvarum
items.description: The most abundant yeast on grape skins. Lemon-shaped (apiculate) cells dominate the first 24-48 hours of wild fermentation. Previously considered purely negative, now recognized for contributing esters and complexity when controlled.
characteristics: Most abundant yeast on healthy grapes (50-75% of population)
Produces acetic acid and ethyl acetate
Dies off quickly as alcohol rises
Requires S. cerevisiae to complete fermentation
items.flavor_contribution: Fruity esters at low levels; acetate and VA at high levels

controversy.traditional_view: Spoilage organism producing volatile acidity and off-flavors
controversy.modern_view: Controlled populations contribute complexity and ester diversity
controversy.research: AWRI studies show moderate Hanseniaspora activity increases ester diversity without excessive VA

items.related_species: Hanseniaspora guilliermondii, H. osmophila (more alcohol tolerant)

items.name: Starmerella bacillaris
items.description: Thrives in high-sugar environments where other yeasts struggle. Naturally associated with botrytized grapes. Produces glycerol and fructophilic fermentation (prefers fructose over glucose).
items.origin: Associated with botrytized and dried grape wines
items.relevance: Important in Sauternes, Tokaji, Trockenbeerenauslese production
items.commercial_use: Not widely commercialized; naturally present in botrytis vineyards
items.fructophilic_note: By preferentially consuming fructose, leaves glucose-dominant residual sugar which tastes less sweet

items.name: Zygosaccharomyces bailii
items.description: The wine industry's most problematic spoilage yeast. Extremely resistant to preservatives, can grow in bottled wine, and survives conditions that kill other yeasts. A nightmare for sweet wine producers.
items.flavor_contribution: Negative: acetic acid, ethyl acetate, off-flavors
items.threat_level: High - causes recalls, bottle fermentation in sweet wines

items.name: Saccharomyces uvarum
items.description: A cold-tolerant Saccharomyces species historically found in cool-climate wine regions. Ferments well at temperatures that stress S. cerevisiae. Being used for hybrid breeding programs.
characteristics: Produces higher glycerol than S. cerevisiae
Natural in Alsace, Germany, cool-climate regions
Used in hybrid breeding with S. cerevisiae
items.origin: Cool-climate wine regions, especially Alsace and Germany
items.flavor_contribution: Floral esters, rose notes, enhanced glycerol
items.hybrid_breeding: S. cerevisiae × S. uvarum hybrids combine cold tolerance with fermentation vigor (Lallemand Cross Evolution series)

strains.title: Commercial Yeast Strains
strains.description: Hundreds of commercial yeast strains are available, each selected for specific fermentation characteristics, flavor profiles, and stress tolerance. Choosing the right strain is a critical winemaking decision.

categories.neutral: Clean fermentation, minimal flavor impact, lets fruit speak
categories.aromatic: Enhances varietal character, produces specific esters and thiols
categories.robust: High alcohol tolerance, stress resistant, finishes difficult fermentations
categories.specialty: Specific applications: sparkling, high residual sugar, stuck fermentation restart

strains.name: EC-1118
strains.description: The world's most widely used wine yeast. Extremely reliable, stress-tolerant, and neutral. The default choice when in doubt.
best_for: Any wine where reliability is priority
strains.popularity: The most used wine yeast globally

strains.name: RC212
strains.description: Selected specifically for Pinot Noir. Enhances color extraction, produces spicy and fruity esters, and promotes complexity. The go-to strain for premium Pinot.

strains.name: D47
strains.description: Aromatic white wine specialist. Enhances tropical and citrus notes, produces rich mouthfeel. Excellent for Chardonnay, Viognier, and aromatic whites. Somewhat temperature sensitive.
strains.caution: Can struggle above 20°C; may produce excessive H2S if nutrient-deficient

strains.name: 71B
strains.description: The semi-sweet wine specialist. Metabolizes malic acid, softening acidity. Produces intense fruit esters. Ideal for nouveau-style wines, rosés, and fruit-forward styles.

strains.name: BM45
strains.description: Selected from Sangiovese fermentations in Montalcino. Respects variety and terroir while enhancing color and structure. The gold standard for Italian reds.

strains.name: QA23
strains.description: The thiol-releasing specialist. Extremely effective at converting grape precursors into tropical and citrus thiols. The choice for Sauvignon Blanc and aromatic whites.

strains.name: Côte des Blancs
strains.description: Leaves residual sweetness due to low alcohol tolerance. Popular for German-style wines, fruit wines, and anywhere off-dry profile is desired.

strains.name: VIN13
strains.description: South African workhorse. Excellent ester production, particularly for Sauvignon Blanc. Enhances tropical character while maintaining freshness.

strains.name: Premier Cuvée
strains.description: Strong fermenter similar to EC-1118. Very clean, reliable, and tolerant of difficult conditions. Popular home winemaking choice.

strains.name: ICV-GRE
strains.description: Grenache specialist. Enhances spicy, peppery notes while maintaining freshness. Excellent for Rhône-style reds and GSM blends.

strains.name: CSM
strains.description: Non-GMO hybrid yeast bred for enhanced aromatic potential. Combines traits of S. cerevisiae and S. uvarum for cold-climate fermentation and thiol release.

strains.name: K1-V1116
strains.description: A competitive strain that dominates fermentations. Killer-positive factor eliminates competing yeasts. Excellent for fresh aromatic whites where it enhances floral and tropical notes.

strains.name: DV10
strains.description: Premium Champagne yeast for secondary fermentation. Extremely cold-tolerant, pressure-resistant, and produces fine, persistent bubbles. The choice for méthode traditionnelle.

strains.name: D254
strains.description: Red wine specialist bred for structured, age-worthy wines. Enhances color extraction, produces intense fruit with spicy notes. Popular for Syrah and Rhône varieties.
characteristics: Intense spice and dark fruit esters

strains.name: CY3079
strains.description: Selected from grand cru Chardonnay fermentations in Chablis. Produces elegant, mineral whites with restrained fruit. The choice for Burgundian-style Chardonnay.

strains.name: BDX
strains.description: Bordeaux specialist selected for Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot. Enhances varietal character, color stability, and structured tannins. Built for age-worthy reds.

strains.name: W15
strains.description: Selected specifically for low H2S production. Ideal for challenging musts with low YAN or high sulfate soils. Prevents reductive characters.
wine_styles: Any style where reduction is a concern

strains.name: SVG
strains.description: Alsace specialist selected for aromatic varieties. Enhances terpenes and floral notes. Produces expressive Gewürztraminer, Riesling, and Muscat.

strains.name: Assmanshausen
strains.description: Classic German Spätburgunder (Pinot Noir) strain. Selected from the famous Assmannshausen vineyards. Produces silky, aromatic Pinot with red fruit character.
strains.historic_note: Assmannshausen has grown Pinot Noir since the 12th century

strains.name: NT116
strains.description: Natural high SO2 producer. Protects wine during fermentation and early aging without additions. Useful for reducing sulfite use in low-intervention winemaking.

selection.title: Yeast Selection Criteria
selection.description: Choosing yeast requires matching strain characteristics to grape, style, and fermentation conditions. No single strain is best for everything.

factors.description: The most important consideration. What character do you want in the finished wine?
Must match expected final alcohol. High-sugar musts need high-tolerance yeasts.
Must match fermentation temperature you can achieve/maintain.
considerations: Warm (25-30°C): Faster, more extraction, stress risk

factors.description: Low-nitrogen musts need low-nutrient yeasts to avoid stuck fermentation.
Fast fermenters for efficiency; slow for controlled extraction and complexity.
How well yeast settles after fermentation. High flocculation = cleaner lees, easier riddling.
factors.relevance: Critical for sparkling wine (riddling) and clarity

fermentation.title: Fermentation Science
fermentation.description: Fermentation is a complex biochemical process. Understanding the science helps prevent problems and optimize quality.

stages.description: Yeast acclimates to must conditions. Cell division begins. No visible fermentation yet.
stages.what_happens: Yeast synthesizes enzymes, builds cell membranes, prepares for growth
stages.concerns: Extended lag = risk of spoilage organisms, oxidation
stages.management: Proper rehydration, yeast nutrients, temperature control

stages.description: Rapid yeast multiplication. Vigorous fermentation begins. CO2 production visible.
stages.what_happens: Cell count doubles every 2-4 hours. Sugar consumption accelerates. Temperature rises.
stages.management: Temperature control, space for foam, nutrient additions

stages.description: Growth slows as nutrients deplete. Fermentation continues but cell division stops.
stages.what_happens: Yeast maintains activity using stored reserves. Alcohol accumulates.
stages.management: Monitor sugar consumption, nutrient additions if needed

stages.description: Sugar exhausted or alcohol toxic. Yeast dies and settles as lees.
stages.what_happens: Autolysis releases compounds; can add complexity (sur lie aging)
stages.management: Timely racking or intentional lees contact depending on style

stuck_fermentation.title: Stuck Fermentation
stuck_fermentation.description: When fermentation stops before all sugar is consumed. One of the most common winemaking problems.

causes.detail: Osmotic stress from very high Brix
Wild killer strain eliminated inoculated yeast

causes.solution: Avoid late sprays, use hardy yeast

prevention: Measure and adjust YAN before fermentation
Use appropriate yeast for expected alcohol
Add nutrients in stages (not all at once)
restart_protocol: Prepare vigorous starter with fresh yeast (EC-1118)
Gradually acclimate starter to stuck wine

temperature.title: Temperature Effects

ranges.effect: Very slow fermentation, maximum aromatic preservation, risk of stuck
Slow fermentation, good aromatic preservation, balanced
Moderate fermentation, standard for most wines
Fast fermentation, more extraction, stress risk
Stress zone, risk of stuck fermentation, off-flavors

temperature.science: Higher temperatures increase yeast metabolism but also stress. Volatiles escape more at warm temperatures. Cold fermentation preserves aromatics but slows kinetics.

nutrients.title: Yeast Nutrients
nutrients.description: Yeast needs nitrogen, vitamins, and minerals beyond sugar to ferment successfully.

yan.title: YAN (Yeast Assimilable Nitrogen)
yan.description: The nitrogen available for yeast to use. Critical for fermentation health.

additions.notes: Cheap, effective, but excessive use can stress yeast
Preferred by yeast, more complex nutrition

nutrients.timing: Best in 2-3 additions: at lag phase, 1/3 sugar depletion, 2/3 sugar depletion. Never add after 2/3 depletion.

oxygen.title: Oxygen Management
oxygen.description: Yeast requires some oxygen despite being a 'anaerobic' process. Understanding when to provide and exclude oxygen is critical.

yeast_oxygen_needs.rehydration: Critical. Active dry yeast needs 5-10 mg/L O2 during rehydration for membrane synthesis. Use aerated water or commercial rehydration nutrients.
yeast_oxygen_needs.lag_phase: Helpful. Small amounts of oxygen help yeast build healthy cell membranes (sterols, unsaturated fatty acids).
yeast_oxygen_needs.active_fermentation: Generally excluded. CO2 production creates protective blanket. Excessive O2 wastes yeast energy and can produce acetaldhyde.
yeast_oxygen_needs.late_fermentation: Some strains benefit from micro-oxygenation for completing fermentation.

rehydration_protocol.nutrients: Go-Ferm or similar rehydration nutrient recommended
rehydration_protocol.acclimation: Slowly add must to starter before inoculation to acclimate to temperature

cap_management.title: Cap Management (Red Wine)
cap_management.description: During red wine fermentation, skins and solids float to form a 'cap.' Managing this cap affects extraction and wine quality.

methods.description: Manually or mechanically pushing cap down into wine
Pumping wine from bottom over cap
Draining wine off, letting cap fall, splashing back
Horizontal rotating tank keeps cap mixed
Screen holds cap below surface throughout

mlf_interaction.title: MLF Interaction with Alcoholic Fermentation
mlf_interaction.description: Malolactic fermentation (MLF) converts sharp malic acid to softer lactic acid. Timing relative to alcoholic fermentation matters.

co_inoculation.description: Add MLB at same time as yeast or 24-48 hours later
disadvantages: Harder to prevent MLF if unwanted

compatibility.yeast_strain_matters: Some yeasts inhibit MLB (high SO2 production, competitive exclusion). Others are 'MLF-compatible.'

mlf_interaction.nutrients_for_mlf: Yeast consumes nutrients MLB needs. Consider bacterial nutrients if sequential MLF is slow.

compounds.title: Flavor Compounds
compounds.description: Yeast produces hundreds of compounds beyond ethanol. These shape wine character as much as grape variety. Understanding yeast biochemistry is essential for managing wine flavor.

overview.total_compounds: Fermentation produces 400+ volatile compounds in addition to ethanol and CO2
overview.concentration_matters: Most compounds have threshold concentrations below which they're not perceived. The interaction between compounds creates overall 'flavor integration.'

categories.name: Esters
categories.description: Fruity, floral aromas. The primary positive contribution of fermentation.
categories.formation: Enzymatic reaction between alcohols and organic acids

categories.name: Higher Alcohols (Fusel Oils)
categories.description: Complex alcohols heavier than ethanol. Moderate levels add body; excess causes harshness.
categories.aroma_character: Solvent-like at high levels, adds complexity at low levels
categories.balance: Higher alcohols are precursors to esters, so some formation is necessary

categories.name: Aldehydes
categories.description: Reactive compounds, mostly oxidized away during fermentation. Acetaldehyde is key.
categories.formation: Oxidation of alcohols, intermediate in ethanol production

examples.notes: Key Sherry character; fault in table wine

categories.significance: Acetaldehyde binds SO2, reducing free SO2. Also involved in color stability.

categories.name: Sulfur Compounds
categories.description: Mostly negative (reductive). Produced under stress, particularly nitrogen deficiency.
categories.aroma_character: Rotten egg, rubber, struck match, garlic
categories.prevention: Adequate YAN, proper temperature, healthy yeast

categories.name: Glycerol
categories.description: A polyol (sugar alcohol) that adds body and sweetness perception.
categories.formation: Byproduct of glycolysis, especially under stress
categories.levels: Typically 5-15 g/L; higher in botrytized wines

categories.name: Volatile Phenols
categories.description: Phenolic compounds, mostly from Brettanomyces. Also medicinal notes from some yeasts.
categories.formation: Enzymatic conversion of grape-derived precursors (hydroxycinnamic acids)
categories.pof_positive_yeasts: Some S. cerevisiae strains have active POF (phenolic off-flavor) genes that produce vinyl phenols. These strains are avoided for wine but valued in Belgian wheat beers.

categories.name: Varietal Thiols
categories.description: Sulfur-containing compounds responsible for tropical and citrus aromas in Sauvignon Blanc, Colombard, and other varieties. Not produced de novo by yeast but released from odorless grape precursors.
categories.formation: Yeast enzymes (carbon-sulfur lyases) cleave cysteine or glutathione conjugates in grapes, releasing volatile thiols
categories.aroma_character: Tropical fruit, passionfruit, grapefruit, citrus, boxwood
categories.importance: Thiols define the character of modern Sauvignon Blanc. Marlborough style depends on thiol release.

key_compounds.notes: Extremely potent, present in Sauvignon Blanc and Scheurebe

yeast_differences.mechanism: Yeast strains differ in carbon-sulfur lyase activity and ability to acetylate 3-MH to 3-MHA

categories.name: Organic Acids
categories.description: Yeast produces and modifies organic acids during fermentation. These affect wine acidity, flavor, and stability.
categories.formation: Byproducts of amino acid metabolism, TCA cycle, and other pathways

examples.notes: Always produced in small amounts. Excessive = volatile acidity fault.
More from MLF than yeast. Lachancea thermotolerans produces significant amounts.
Intermediate in glycolysis. Binds SO2. Higher in stuck fermentations.

malic_acid_modification.by_yeast: Some strains (71B) metabolize 20-40% of malic acid during fermentation
malic_acid_modification.schizo_pombe: S. pombe converts malic acid to ethanol (maloalcoholic fermentation)

categories.name: Terpene Modification
categories.description: Terpenes come from grapes, but yeast enzymes modify them. Glycosidase enzymes release bound terpenes; other enzymes convert between forms.
categories.formation: Yeast β-glucosidases cleave sugar-bound (glycosylated) terpene precursors, releasing volatile aromatic forms

yeast_effects.glycosidase_activity: Varies by strain. Some yeasts release more bound terpenes than others.
yeast_effects.biotransformation: Yeast can convert one terpene to another (e.g., geraniol → citronellol)

techniques.title: Fermentation Techniques
techniques.description: How fermentation is managed reflects winemaking philosophy and style goals.

techniques.name: Indigenous (Wild) Fermentation
techniques.description: Relying on naturally-present yeasts from vineyard, winery, and equipment. No commercial inoculation.
process: Wait for natural yeast activity to begin
Multiple yeast species active in sequence
techniques.who_uses: Natural wine producers, traditional Burgundy, Loire, some biodynamic estates

techniques.name: Inoculated Fermentation
techniques.description: Adding commercial yeast to control fermentation. The standard approach globally.
process: Add sulfite to suppress wild yeasts

techniques.name: Co-Inoculation
techniques.description: Adding malolactic bacteria at the same time as yeast, rather than after alcoholic fermentation.
process: MLF often completes before or with AF
disadvantages: Potential for diacetyl if not managed
techniques.who_uses: Increasingly popular for reds and full-bodied whites

techniques.name: Sequential Non-Saccharomyces
techniques.description: Inoculating with non-Saccharomyces yeast first, then S. cerevisiae to finish.
process: Inoculate S. cerevisiae to complete fermentation
techniques.who_uses: Premium aromatic white producers, experimental winemakers

techniques.name: Sur Lie Aging
techniques.description: Aging wine on dead yeast cells (lees) after fermentation. Yeast autolysis releases compounds.
disadvantages: Risk of off-flavors if lees not healthy
techniques.styles: Muscadet sur lie, Champagne, premium Burgundy white

techniques.name: Carbonic Maceration
techniques.description: Whole grape fermentation in CO2 atmosphere. Intracellular fermentation without yeast involvement initially.
process: Whole clusters placed in CO2-filled tank
Eventually grapes break, yeast takes over
techniques.styles: Beaujolais Nouveau, some Loire, experimental wines

techniques.name: Pied de Cuve
techniques.description: Building a starter culture from local grapes before main harvest. Captures indigenous yeasts.
process: Harvest early-ripening grapes or selected vines
Use active fermenting must to inoculate main harvest
techniques.who_uses: Traditional Burgundy, natural wine producers seeking reliability

techniques.name: Cold Soak (Pre-fermentation Maceration)
techniques.description: Holding crushed red grapes at cold temperatures before fermentation begins. Extracts color and flavor without tannin.
process: Warm and inoculate to begin fermentation
techniques.mechanism: Cold temperature inhibits yeast activity but allows aqueous extraction. Color (anthocyanins) extracts easily; tannin requires alcohol.
disadvantages: Risk of spoilage if temperature control fails
techniques.who_uses: Burgundy producers, Oregon Pinot, cool-climate regions

techniques.name: Extended Maceration (Post-fermentation)
techniques.description: Leaving wine on skins after fermentation completes. Continued extraction and tannin polymerization.
process: Keep wine on skins for additional 1-8 weeks
Minimal punch-downs to avoid harsh extraction
techniques.mechanism: Alcohol continues extracting tannin, but polymerization (chain lengthening) also occurs, softening tannins over time.
techniques.who_uses: Bordeaux (traditional), Australia (Barossa Shiraz), serious Rhône reds
techniques.monitoring: Taste daily to assess tannin development

techniques.name: Whole Cluster Fermentation
techniques.description: Including grape stems in fermentation. Adds structure, spice, and freshness but risks green characters.
process: Destem some clusters, keep others whole
Layer whole clusters with crushed fruit
Partial carbonic maceration occurs inside whole berries
techniques.stem_ripeness: Critical. Only works with fully lignified (brown, woody) stems. Green stems = green flavors.
techniques.percentage: 10-100% whole cluster depending on vintage and style
techniques.who_uses: Traditional Burgundy (DRC uses 100%), Rhône, Oregon, natural wine producers

techniques.name: Délestage (Rack and Return)
techniques.description: Draining fermenting wine off skins, aerating, and returning. French technique for color and soft tannins.
process: Drain fermenting wine from tank, leaving cap
Cap falls and compacts under own weight
techniques.mechanism: Aeration promotes color stability (acetaldehyde bridges anthocyanins). Cap compression then expansion improves extraction.
techniques.vs_pumping_over: More gentle than pump-over, more oxygenating than punch-down
techniques.who_uses: Premium Bordeaux, California Cabernet, quality-focused producers

techniques.name: Micro-oxygenation During Fermentation
techniques.description: Controlled addition of tiny amounts of oxygen during fermentation. Promotes color stability and tannin polymerization.
process: Ceramic diffuser introduces micro-bubbles of O2
techniques.mechanism: Oxygen promotes acetaldehyde formation, which bridges anthocyanins to tannins (color stability). Also polymerizes tannins (softening).
techniques.who_uses: Modern wineries worldwide, particularly for early-drinking reds

modern.title: Modern Developments
modern.description: Yeast science is advancing rapidly. Non-GMO breeding, non-Saccharomyces commercialization, and climate adaptation are current frontiers.

trends.description: Previously considered spoilage organisms, non-Saccharomyces yeasts are now valued for specific contributions.
trends.products: Biodiva, Flavia, Laktia, Frootzen, and others

trends.description: Traditional crossing of yeast strains to combine traits without genetic modification.
examples: S. cerevisiae × S. uvarum hybrids for cold tolerance

trends.description: Warmer vintages produce higher-sugar, lower-acid musts. Yeast selection helps compensate.
challenges: Higher potential alcohol requiring tolerant strains
solutions: Strains that produce less alcohol per gram sugar

trends.description: Capturing and commercializing indigenous yeasts from specific vineyards or regions.
trends.philosophy: Combining indigenous character with commercial reliability
trends.programs: Several universities and companies offer strain isolation services

trends.description: Understanding yeast genetics enables targeted breeding and potentially GMO applications.
trends.gmo_status: GMO wine yeasts exist (ML01) but have minimal commercial adoption due to consumer resistance
trends.future: Non-GMO breeding guided by genomic knowledge

research_institutions.name: UC Davis
research_institutions.focus: Yeast genetics, strain development, fermentation science
research_institutions.notable: Origin of many commercial strains (Montrachet, Prise de Mousse). Leading American wine research center.

research_institutions.name: AWRI (Australian Wine Research Institute)
research_institutions.focus: Non-Saccharomyces, thiol research, practical winemaking applications
research_institutions.notable: Leading non-Saccharomyces research. Pioneered understanding of thiol release mechanisms.

research_institutions.name: INRAE (formerly INRA)
research_institutions.focus: Indigenous yeast, terroir expression, strain diversity, breeding
research_institutions.notable: Source of ICV strain series (D47, D254, GRE). French national research institute.

research_institutions.name: Stellenbosch University / IWBT
research_institutions.focus: Yeast genomics, non-Saccharomyces, flavor compound production
research_institutions.notable: Institute for Wine Biotechnology. Major yeast genetics research center.

research_institutions.name: Geisenheim University
research_institutions.focus: Cool-climate fermentation, Riesling specialization, yeast-bacteria interactions
research_institutions.notable: World's oldest wine research institute (founded 1872). Strong ML and yeast tradition.

research_institutions.name: University of Adelaide
research_institutions.focus: Yeast stress response, fermentation optimization, wine quality
research_institutions.notable: Strong collaboration with AWRI. Focus on practical winemaking applications.

research_institutions.name: Cornell University
research_institutions.focus: Cool-climate viticulture, hybrid grapes, yeast selection for difficult conditions
research_institutions.notable: Focus on New York and cool-climate regions. Pioneering cold-hardy grape breeding.

research_institutions.name: University of Bordeaux / ISVV
research_institutions.focus: Bordeaux varieties, MLF, yeast-wine chemistry interactions
research_institutions.notable: Institut des Sciences de la Vigne et du Vin. Close ties to Bordeaux wine industry.

research_institutions.name: Università di Firenze
research_institutions.focus: Sangiovese fermentation, non-Saccharomyces, indigenous Italian strains
research_institutions.notable: Strong ties to Tuscany wine industry. BM45 development partner.

research_institutions.name: Universidad de la República
research_institutions.focus: Tannat fermentation, warm-climate challenges, local strain isolation
research_institutions.notable: Leading South American wine research center.

future_directions.description: Strains that produce less ethanol per gram of sugar. Critical for climate change adaptation.
approaches: Diverting carbon to glycerol instead of ethanol

future_directions.description: Breeding for strains that maximize tropical/citrus thiol conversion from grape precursors.
future_directions.mechanism: Selecting for high carbon-sulfur lyase activity

future_directions.description: Using sensor technology and AI to optimize fermentation in real-time.
Strain-specific nutrient protocols for optimized fermentation health.
future_directions.concept: Different strains have different nutrient needs; matching nutrients to strain optimizes performance

future_directions.description: Using yeast and bacteria to protect must without SO2. Growing interest for low-sulfite winemaking.
future_directions.mechanism: Non-Saccharomyces yeasts consume oxygen and nutrients, outcompeting spoilage organisms

future_directions.description: Precise genetic modification for specific traits without introducing foreign DNA.
future_directions.controversy: Consumer acceptance remains uncertain. Some argue CRISPR-edited organisms are not 'GMO' in traditional sense.

future_directions.description: Using DNA sequencing to characterize vineyard and winery microbiomes.
future_directions.future: May enable 'microbial terroir' marketing and authentication

suppliers.name: Anchor Oenology (Lallemand subsidiary)
suppliers.market_position: South African and New World focus
